paragraphs.en: Welcome to Signals & Threads, in-depth conversations about every layer of the tech stack from Jane Street. I'm your host, Ron Minsky. My guest today is Greta Yorsh. Greta is a researcher and engineer here at Jane Street working on compiler technology. Welcome, Greta.
paragraphs.zh: 欢迎收听 Signals & Threads，这是来自 Jane Street 的播客，我们会深入探讨技术栈的每一层。我是主持人 Ron Minsky。今天的嘉宾是 Greta Yorsh。Greta 是 Jane Street 的研究员和工程师，从事编译器技术方面的工作。欢迎你，Greta。

paragraphs.en: I studied in Israel. I did my undergraduate studies in computer science and economics. I found that I liked the theoretical aspects of computer science more than the engineering aspects, and went to do graduate studies in Hebrew University, and my graduate research was on software verification and more specifically on abstract interpretation.
paragraphs.zh: 我在以色列读书。我的本科读的是计算机科学和经济学。我发现比起工程方面，我更喜欢计算机科学中理论性的部分，于是去希伯来大学读了研究生，我的研究生研究方向是软件验证，更具体地说是抽象解释。

paragraphs.en: Tell us a little bit more about how that fits into the arc of your research.
paragraphs.zh: 跟我们多讲讲这是怎么融入你整个研究历程的吧。

paragraphs.en: I started my research working on the problem of software verification or certain aspects of this problem. And I think what attracted me was the combination of using formal methods, using mathematical models, logical reasoning, but in a practical context of a programming language. So it was this combination of beautiful mathematics and applied goal, and that got me hooked.
paragraphs.zh: 我研究的起点是软件验证问题，或者说这个问题的某些方面。我想吸引我的地方在于：它把形式化方法、数学模型、逻辑推理结合在一起，却又置身于编程语言这样一个实际的语境中。所以这是一种把优美的数学和应用目标结合起来的东西，我一下就被它迷住了。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, it's interesting, you're starting off by focusing on verification, because I think verification is something that people in industry often think of and talk about as a holy grail kind of thing where it would be wonderful, but in practice it seems like it's never really taken off. What's your view of the current state of verification?
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，有意思的是，你一开始就专注于验证，因为我觉得验证这个东西，工业界的人经常会把它当成一种圣杯来看待、来谈论——它要是能实现就太棒了，但实际上它似乎从来没有真正流行起来。你怎么看验证目前的状况？

paragraphs.en: The kind of verification, proving properties of programs, that I think of when I say software verification, this is research still done today. And in fact, the application of tools like formal verification and static analysis to hardware design has been for many years, very productive. Microprocessors, FPGAs, all of these kinds of hardware design use a lot of static analysis and formal verification tools to check correctness.
paragraphs.zh: 我说软件验证时所想到的那种验证——证明程序的性质——这种研究今天仍然有人在做。事实上，把形式化验证和静态分析这类工具应用到硬件设计上，多年来一直很有成效。微处理器、FPGA，所有这类硬件设计都大量使用静态分析和形式化验证工具来检查正确性。

paragraphs.en: It's interesting for you to say this thing about the way in which tests and formal proofs mix together and interact with each other. Because I think it's often presented as tests versus formal proof. In practice, what you're saying is, they actually interact in really interesting ways.
paragraphs.zh: 你说测试和形式化证明这两者是如何混合、相互作用的，这点很有意思。因为我觉得人们经常把它呈现为测试对阵形式化证明。但实际上，按你说的，它们其实是以非常有趣的方式相互作用的。

paragraphs.en: So, one of the ways that I think is quite… in its own, it gives understanding of the formal reasoning. It's the kind of techniques that use tests to help drive formal reasoning. So, you start with a test, and then, in some magical way, using some formal technique, that test is converted to a proof. And this is done in practice and in research. These are really popular techniques. The key insight is that tests are very easy to write. Formal proofs are generally very hard to write. In some sense, they're difficult to read, too. So, if you can leverage the easy part – writing a test – but get more out of it – getting a proof – that seems great. And there are many ways to do this.
paragraphs.zh: 有一种方式，我觉得它本身就……它让你理解形式化推理。就是那种用测试来帮助驱动形式化推理的技术。你从一个测试开始，然后以某种神奇的方式，用某种形式化技术，把那个测试转化成一个证明。这在实践中和研究中都有人做。这些都是非常流行的技术。关键的洞见在于：测试非常容易写，而形式化证明通常很难写。某种意义上，它们也很难读。所以，如果你能利用容易的那部分——写测试——但从中获得更多——得到一个证明——那看起来就很棒。而做到这一点有很多方法。

paragraphs.en: So, let me see if I understand this correctly. It sounds like what you're doing is you start with an example, which says in this particular case, with this particular input, I should get this output. And then, somehow, generalize that to say, for any input of this type, here's the property that should hold. There's something that does that generalization and magic that gets you from the specific to the general. What is that thing?
paragraphs.zh: 那让我看看我有没有理解对。听起来你做的事情是：你从一个例子开始，它说在这个特定情况下、用这个特定输入，我应该得到这个输出。然后，以某种方式把它泛化，说对于这种类型的任何输入，这里有一个应该成立的性质。有某种东西完成了那种泛化、那种从特定到一般的魔法。那东西是什么？

paragraphs.en: It's not one technique. There are many techniques, but this is the general way in which I think they work. You start with a test, and then you go through the execution, and you can identify many things. On the way, you can identify invariants, relationships between variables, all kinds of conditions that must hold. And then you have to determine which ones are useful for making the proof.
paragraphs.zh: 这不是单一的一种技术。有很多技术，但这就是我认为它们大致的工作方式。你从一个测试开始，然后过一遍执行过程，你能识别出很多东西。在这个过程中，你可以识别出不变量、变量之间的关系、各种必须成立的条件。然后你得判断哪些对完成证明是有用的。

paragraphs.en: Got it. So, these are essentially all ways of kind of popping back and forth between the formal methods world and the testing world, and using each to improve the other.
paragraphs.zh: 明白了。所以这些本质上都是在形式化方法世界和测试世界之间来回跳转的方式，并用其中一个去改进另一个。

paragraphs.en: And more precisely. This combination, I think it's beautiful mathematically, but there are so many ways in which you can try. And actually, many of these programs could be the source of a new research problem.
paragraphs.zh: 更确切地说，这种结合，我觉得它在数学上很优美，但你能尝试的方式实在太多了。而且实际上，其中很多程序本身都可能成为一个新研究问题的来源。

paragraphs.en: To step back for a moment, it sounds like some of your frustration with working on this verification was that the practical impact was limited. Is that a fair reading?
paragraphs.zh: 退一步说，听起来你做这个验证工作时的一部分挫败感在于，它的实际影响有限。这样理解对吗？

paragraphs.en: Yes.
paragraphs.zh: 是的。

paragraphs.en: This leads me to ask about something that you've worked on, which is superoptimization, which is an interesting technique that in some sense marries optimization and verification. Can you tell me what superoptimization is and why it's interesting?
paragraphs.zh: 这让我想问你做过的一个东西，就是超优化，这是一种有趣的技术，某种意义上把优化和验证结合在了一起。你能告诉我超优化是什么，以及它为什么有意思吗？

paragraphs.en: So, let me tell you a story first. So, when I worked at ARM on the ARM compiler backend, there is interesting interaction between the hardware engineers and the software engineers. So, the hardware engineers are designing new instructions for the hardware, and the software engineers need to then modify the compiler to use these instructions effectively. And the collaboration is slow, because the hardware engineers want to evaluate the new instruction and the compiler team needs to be involved in this, and this slows down the process.
paragraphs.zh: 那我先讲个故事吧。我在 ARM 做 ARM 编译器后端的时候，硬件工程师和软件工程师之间有很有意思的互动。硬件工程师在为硬件设计新指令，而软件工程师则需要修改编译器，让它能有效地使用这些指令。而这种协作很慢，因为硬件工程师想要评估新指令，编译器团队又必须参与进来，这就拖慢了整个过程。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, a little more mechanically, if you could just describe how superoptimization works?
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，能不能更具体一点，描述一下超优化是怎么工作的？

paragraphs.en: In superoptimization, you have an instruction sequence, and the goal is to find another instruction sequence that is equivalent to it but shorter or faster. And there are basically two components. One is the search process to find candidates – potential improvements. The other component is verifying that this candidate is correct, that it is equivalent to the original instruction sequence.
paragraphs.zh: 在超优化中，你有一个指令序列，目标是找到另一个与它等价但更短或更快的指令序列。基本上有两个组成部分。一个是搜索过程，用来找候选项——潜在的改进。另一个组成部分是验证这个候选项是正确的，即它和原始指令序列是等价的。

paragraphs.en: It sounds like this all connects to two very different models of how you think about building a compiler. One model is to say we're going to hand-code all of our optimizations. The compiler engineers are going to sit down and think hard about what the right transformations are, and they're going to write them down. The other model is to have the compiler figure out optimizations by just doing search. What's your view of this tension?
paragraphs.zh: 听起来这一切都连接到关于如何构建编译器的两种非常不同的模型。一种模型是说，我们要手工编写所有的优化。编译器工程师会坐下来认真思考什么才是正确的变换，然后把它们写下来。另一种模型是让编译器仅仅通过搜索来找出优化。你怎么看这两者之间的张力？

paragraphs.en: It's a really interesting question. I would like to see it used more. I think it depends on how fast the search is. Today, superoptimization is used for small sequences, short instruction sequences. The longer the instruction sequence, the less practical it becomes. The key thing that determines how fast the search is, is how quickly you can verify that the candidate is correct. And there is some very interesting research on speeding up this verification, but it hasn't yet gotten to the point where it can be applied to much longer instruction sequences.
paragraphs.zh: 这是个非常有意思的问题。我希望看到它被更多地使用。我觉得这取决于搜索有多快。如今，超优化用于小的序列、短的指令序列。指令序列越长，它就越不实用。决定搜索快慢的关键因素，是你能多快地验证候选项是正确的。在加速这种验证方面有一些非常有意思的研究，但它还没有达到能应用于长得多的指令序列的程度。

paragraphs.en: So is your point that superoptimization improves the collaboration cycle between the hardware engineer and the compiler engineer? Basically, if you can, with less human effort, figure out what the right transformations are for a new instruction, the hardware engineers can more freely explore instruction design, and you get a better result?
paragraphs.zh: 那么你的意思是，超优化改善了硬件工程师和编译器工程师之间的协作循环？基本上就是，如果你能用更少的人力找出对一条新指令而言正确的变换，硬件工程师就能更自由地探索指令设计，你就能得到更好的结果？

paragraphs.en: So that's one of the ways in which superoptimization already, today, can help, I think. It can help this collaboration because some of the work of figuring out what the new instruction can do is just automatically done. So the compiler has to be less involved, and more time is available for evaluation.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这就是超优化如今已经能帮上忙的方式之一。它能帮助这种协作，因为搞清楚新指令能做什么的部分工作就自动完成了。所以编译器需要参与的就更少，可以有更多时间用于评估。

paragraphs.en: The idea of superoptimization as a way of helping people who are building the core infrastructure seems like it could connect to something here at Jane Street, which is thinking about our DSLs. Where do you think it is or isn't useful as a way of thinking about what we should do in terms of DSL optimization?
paragraphs.zh: 把超优化当作一种帮助那些构建核心基础设施的人的思路，似乎能和 Jane Street 这里的某件事联系起来，那就是思考我们的 DSL。在思考我们在 DSL 优化方面该做什么时，你觉得它在哪些地方有用、哪些地方没用？

paragraphs.en: So, I think when you decide on your DSL and your hardware, then you need to kind of make that expensive investment in building good code generation for your DSL. And superoptimization may be helpful for this because you can kind of experiment with the design of the DSL or the hardware more quickly. You can explore a bigger design space. And the benefit is you need less human time and expertise to find good instruction sequences.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得当你确定了你的 DSL 和你的硬件之后，你就需要做那笔昂贵的投入，为你的 DSL 构建良好的代码生成。而超优化对此可能有帮助，因为你可以更快地实验 DSL 或硬件的设计。你可以探索更大的设计空间。好处是你需要更少的人力时间和专业知识来找到好的指令序列。

paragraphs.en: So, let's switch gears. I want to talk a little bit about how you got to where you are now. You have this interesting background where you come out of this sort of formal methods and verification world, and then you went to ARM and worked on compiler backends, and now you're at Jane Street. Talk a little bit about the trajectory of that, and also why Jane Street?
paragraphs.zh: 那我们换个话题。我想聊一点你是怎么走到现在这一步的。你有一段有意思的经历：你从形式化方法和验证那个世界出来，然后去了 ARM 做编译器后端，现在又在 Jane Street。聊聊这个轨迹吧，还有，为什么选 Jane Street？

paragraphs.en: So, I feel like, through the research, I because familiar with a set of powerful tools that can be combined in interesting and useful ways for solving compiler problems. And I wanted to apply this to industrial settings because I wanted to have impact. And the value for me of working at Jane Street, I think, is that I get to focus on the parts that I find interesting and to combine the research and engineering aspects in a way that I don't think I could do in many other places.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得，通过研究，我熟悉了一套强大的工具，它们可以以有趣而有用的方式组合起来解决编译器问题。我想把这个应用到工业场景中，因为我想产生影响。而对我来说，在 Jane Street 工作的价值在于，我能专注于我觉得有趣的部分，并以一种我认为在很多其他地方都做不到的方式，把研究和工程两方面结合起来。

paragraphs.en: So, part of what I'm hearing from you is that the thing that excites you about the industrial context is you can see pretty quickly whether or not the improvements that you're making are real, because you have this real system sitting right there that you can actually optimize against?
paragraphs.zh: 那么我从你这里听到的一部分是，让你对工业语境感到兴奋的，是你能相当快地看出你做的改进是不是真实的，因为你眼前就摆着一个真实的系统，你可以真的针对它去优化？

paragraphs.en: One big project I was involved in at Jane Street is doing profile-guided optimizations in OCaml. We have talked about this on the podcast before, so I won't go into all the details, but can you give us a brief overview of what FDO or PGO is and then tell us about your experience with it?
paragraphs.zh: 我在 Jane Street 参与的一个大项目是在 OCaml 中做基于性能剖析的优化。我们以前在播客里聊过这个，所以我就不展开所有细节了，不过你能给我们简要介绍一下 FDO 或 PGO 是什么，然后讲讲你在这方面的经历吗？

paragraphs.en: I remember when we first started working on this and especially when you saw some of the early results, you seemed genuinely shocked at the size of the performance improvements. Can you tell me why those improvements were so large?
paragraphs.zh: 我记得我们刚开始做这个的时候，尤其是你看到一些早期结果的时候，你似乎对性能提升的幅度真的很震惊。你能告诉我为什么那些提升会那么大吗？

paragraphs.en: When the functions that call each other often are far away, when the hardware executes the code, it needs to load all of the code for all of these functions, which leads to cache pressure, larger instruction cache footprint. By having a layout that respects the calling patterns of your code, functions that often call each other are placed closer together, and the overall instruction cache footprint is reduced, and you can have more code in the instruction cache at any given time, which leads to fewer cache misses, which leads to faster execution.
paragraphs.zh: 当那些经常互相调用的函数离得很远时，硬件执行代码时就需要加载所有这些函数的全部代码，这会导致缓存压力、更大的指令缓存占用。通过采用一种尊重你代码调用模式的布局，经常互相调用的函数被放得更近，整体的指令缓存占用就减小了，在任意给定时刻你就能在指令缓存里放更多代码，这会带来更少的缓存未命中，从而带来更快的执行。

paragraphs.en: Right, because if it's predictable and you know whether or not your changes are real changes to the performance of the system, you can more aggressively try to improve things.
paragraphs.zh: 对，因为如果它是可预测的，而且你知道你的改动是不是对系统性能的真实改动，你就可以更激进地去尝试改进。

paragraphs.en: So, this, in fact, ties in with our previous discussion about benchmarks. So there are some people in the research community who think about this problem of the non-determinism in benchmarks and the noise in benchmarks, and how do you know that a difference you measured is actually a real difference and not just noise. And there is research in this direction of, which I'll call, stabilizing benchmarks or making benchmarks more deterministic. And I was trying to apply this to Jane Street for a while. It didn't get very far, which I was surprised about. But I wonder if the noisiness of our benchmarks is actually a good thing. Because the noisiness reflects hardware and system-level things that we don't control and that are real.
paragraphs.zh: 这其实和我们之前关于基准测试的讨论连上了。研究界有些人在思考基准测试中的非确定性和噪声这个问题，以及你怎么知道你测出的差异其实是真实差异而不只是噪声。这方面有一个研究方向，我姑且称之为稳定基准测试，或者说让基准测试更确定。我有一阵子试着把这个应用到 Jane Street，没怎么推进下去，这让我挺意外的。但我在想，我们基准测试的噪声性也许其实是件好事。因为这种噪声反映了我们无法控制、但又真实存在的硬件和系统层面的东西。

paragraphs.en: That's good. So, these are almost the opposite approach, right? One of them is to say let's squish everything down to the core computation and measure that precisely. The other is to say let's let everything be messy and realistic, and see if your change actually wins in that environment.
paragraphs.zh: 很好。所以这几乎是两种相反的思路，对吧？一种是说，我们把所有东西都压缩到核心计算，然后精确地测量它。另一种是说，我们让一切都保持杂乱而真实，然后看看你的改动在那种环境里是不是真的胜出。

paragraphs.en: It's interesting. This research work I mentioned is a way to address this problem, in some sense, by adding information during the execution of the program.
paragraphs.zh: 挺有意思的。我提到的这项研究工作，某种意义上是通过在程序执行期间增加信息来解决这个问题的一种方式。

paragraphs.en: During the execution of the program?
paragraphs.zh: 在程序执行期间？

paragraphs.en: During the execution, yes.
paragraphs.zh: 在执行期间，对。

paragraphs.en: Oh, that's awesome.
paragraphs.zh: 哦，那太棒了。

paragraphs.en: Yes, I know.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，我懂。

paragraphs.en: Another thing to talk about here with FDO is FDO is an interesting kind of example on a spectrum, right? It's on one extreme there's AOT, or ahead-of-time compilation, where you just compile your code once and then you run it. On the other extreme, you have JIT, or just-in-time compilation, where you're doing compilation on the fly as you run the program. And FDO is sort of sitting in the middle, where you're compiling ahead of time but you're using some information about how your program will run to better compile it.
paragraphs.zh: 这里关于 FDO 还有一点要谈的是，FDO 是一个谱系上有意思的例子，对吧？在一个极端是 AOT，即提前编译，你只编译一次代码然后运行它。在另一个极端是 JIT，即即时编译，你在运行程序的同时一边编译。而 FDO 算是坐在中间，你是提前编译的，但你利用了一些关于你的程序将如何运行的信息来更好地编译它。

paragraphs.en: There are many aspects of this, right? So, one is, if you switch to a different hardware, as you said, the profile may not be representative. A similar issue is if your workload changes. The other question is about AOT vs. JIT, and the tradeoffs of each of these. In research, there is interesting work on developing compilers that sit between AOT and JIT.
paragraphs.zh: 这有很多方面，对吧？一个是，如果你换到不同的硬件上，正如你说的，剖析数据可能就不具代表性了。一个类似的问题是，如果你的工作负载变了。另一个问题是关于 AOT 对 JIT，以及它们各自的权衡。在研究领域，有一些有意思的工作在开发介于 AOT 和 JIT 之间的编译器。

paragraphs.en: The core tradeoff being the compiler knows more about the structure and therefore, in some sense, about the best approach for optimizing, whereas the profiling-based approach knows more about the dynamic execution, and in some sense about what's actually hot and actually needs optimization.
paragraphs.zh: 核心的权衡在于：编译器对结构了解得更多，因此某种意义上对最佳优化方法了解得更多；而基于剖析的方法对动态执行了解得更多，某种意义上对什么是真正的热点、什么真正需要优化了解得更多。

paragraphs.en: But also, the same tradeoff. You take it one step back to the source, and again, there are different choices. You could write in a high-level language, and then you have the abstraction, but you give up control to the compiler. Or you could write in a low-level language, or in assembly, where you have control, but you then have less portability, less productivity, and more bugs.
paragraphs.zh: 但也是同样的权衡。你往回退一步到源代码层面，又有不同的选择。你可以用高级语言写，那样你有抽象，但你把控制权交给了编译器。或者你可以用低级语言写，或者用汇编写，那样你有控制权，但随之而来的是更差的可移植性、更低的生产力，以及更多的 bug。

paragraphs.en: Always love visiting and hanging out with the compiler team because there's a ton of exciting stuff going on. Let me shift to talking about OCaml itself as a language to compile for. What's it like working in OCaml as the language of the compiler itself?
paragraphs.zh: 我一直很喜欢来编译器团队转转、待一待，因为这里有一大堆激动人心的事情在发生。让我转到聊 OCaml 本身，作为一门要为之编译的语言。把 OCaml 当作编译器本身所用的语言来工作，是种什么样的体验？

paragraphs.en: Before coming to Jane Street, I have never programmed in OCaml. I knew about the existence of OCaml, but I've never used it. I've never encountered it in any way. And my experience at Jane Street has been very positive. So, the type system, I think, is fantastic, really fantastic for the kind of work that I do in a compiler. Because in a compiler, you have complex data structures, and you have different states of the process, and you have invariants that you need to maintain. And the type system helps with all of this. And I think I appreciate it more and more as I go deeper into the work.
paragraphs.zh: 来 Jane Street 之前，我从来没用 OCaml 编过程。我知道 OCaml 的存在，但我从没用过它。我从没以任何方式接触过它。而我在 Jane Street 的体验非常正面。它的类型系统，我觉得棒极了，对于我在编译器中做的这类工作来说真的棒极了。因为在编译器里，你有复杂的数据结构，你有过程的不同状态，你有需要维护的不变量。而类型系统对所有这些都有帮助。我觉得随着我工作得越深入，我就越来越欣赏它。

paragraphs.en: It's a thing I think that people often don't appreciate who have not used OCaml or a similar language, which is that the type system isn't just an error detection mechanism. It's a way of helping you think about your problem. You kind of think out the structure of your data, and as you do that, things become clearer.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这是没用过 OCaml 或类似语言的人常常体会不到的一点，那就是：类型系统不只是一种错误检测机制。它是一种帮你思考问题的方式。你会去把你数据的结构想清楚，而在你这么做的过程中，事情就变得清晰起来了。

paragraphs.en: This is so true. You know, I have a real story how true it is. I remember a few years ago talking to a junior engineer and showing them how to solve their problem, which was a problem I knew the answer to. And I was guiding them through the types first, before writing any code. And then the code becomes kind of obvious. And they told me afterwards that they were amazed at how easy it was once they saw the types.
paragraphs.zh: 太对了。你知道吗，我有个真实的故事能说明这有多对。我记得几年前跟一个初级工程师聊天，给他演示怎么解决他们的问题，那是个我知道答案的问题。我在写任何代码之前，先带着他们过类型。然后代码就变得有点显而易见了。事后他们告诉我，他们很惊讶，一旦看到了类型，事情竟然那么简单。

paragraphs.en: Okay, so that was all about OCaml as a language to work in. How do you find OCaml as a language to optimize? I.e., are there things about the language that make the compiler's job harder or easier?
paragraphs.zh: 好，那些都是关于把 OCaml 当作工作语言。那你觉得 OCaml 作为一门要去优化的语言怎么样？也就是说，这门语言有没有什么东西让编译器的工作更难或更容易？

paragraphs.en: So, I'm learning to appreciate the value of the choices that were made by OCaml developers regarding the language representation. So, the backend, the representation in the backend is quite low level, and it's, in some sense, closer to assembly than some higher-level intermediate representations that you see in other compilers. And initially, I found this somewhat surprising because I'm used to higher-level IRs in compilers, and that's what I would have expected. But now I understand that there are reasons for this and that it gives a lot of flexibility in the backend and the ability to make choices that otherwise you might not be able to make.
paragraphs.zh: 我正在学着欣赏 OCaml 开发者在语言表示方面所做选择的价值。后端的表示相当低级，某种意义上比你在其他编译器里看到的一些更高级的中间表示更接近汇编。一开始我觉得这有点出乎意料，因为我习惯了编译器里更高级的 IR，那也是我本来会预期的。但现在我明白了，这么做是有原因的，它在后端给了很大的灵活性，以及做出某些否则可能做不到的选择的能力。

paragraphs.en: So that's all about, in some sense, the structure of how the compiler itself is built and how that affects your work. What about the ARM backend? Because I know that's an area that you've looked at a lot. How is that going?
paragraphs.zh: 那些某种意义上都是关于编译器本身是如何构建的结构，以及那如何影响你的工作。那 ARM 后端呢？因为我知道那是你研究了很多的一个领域。进展怎么样？

paragraphs.en: I'm probably not the right person to ask about it, right? But on that subject, I think that, again, and it's related to the point about representation, the structure of the backend, of the ARM backend itself, is not very modular. And modularity there would help, I think.
paragraphs.zh: 我大概不是问这个问题的合适人选，对吧？不过在这个话题上，我觉得，还是那个，这跟表示那一点有关——后端的结构，ARM 后端本身的结构，不是很模块化。我觉得，那里有模块化会有帮助。

paragraphs.en: And actually, let's demystify that terminology. Frontend, middle-end, backend is standard ways people talk about compilers. Can you give us a quick explanation of what those terms mean?
paragraphs.zh: 实际上，我们来把那些术语解释清楚。前端、中端、后端是人们谈论编译器时的标准说法。你能给我们快速解释一下这些术语是什么意思吗？

paragraphs.en: So, you can think of a compiler as applying a sequence of transformations, starting from source code and ending in machine code. And the frontend is the part that takes in source code and converts it to an IR, intermediate representation. And then the backend is the part that converts from IR to the machine code. And the middle-end are the optimizations in between. And the way I think about it is that the frontend and backend are aware of the language, in some sense, and the hardware, respectively, and the middle-end is language and hardware agnostic.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以把编译器想成是在应用一系列变换，从源代码开始，到机器码结束。前端是接收源代码并把它转换成 IR、即中间表示的那部分。然后后端是把 IR 转换成机器码的那部分。而中端则是介于两者之间的优化。我的理解方式是，前端和后端某种意义上分别知晓语言和硬件，而中端是与语言和硬件无关的。

paragraphs.en: Right, and the middle-end is where things, at least in OCaml, where things like inlining occur, and so on. And, and the backend, of course, is interesting at Jane Street because of all of the different environments that people run in.
paragraphs.zh: 对，而中端就是——至少在 OCaml 里——内联这类事情发生的地方，等等。而后端嘛，当然了，在 Jane Street 之所以有意思，是因为人们运行所处的各种不同环境。

paragraphs.en: I have not used OCaml on ARM. I have no experience with it. I've seen the code in the ARM backend to understand the structure but I don't know the behavior in much detail.
paragraphs.zh: 我没在 ARM 上用过 OCaml。我没有这方面的经验。我看过 ARM 后端的代码以理解其结构，但我不太了解它详细的行为。

paragraphs.en: I wonder if this is an area we'll start to care about more as the CPU ecosystem changes, because power efficiency on servers is increasingly becoming an issue, and ARM is the place where the power efficiency seems to be best in class. Is this something you think will become more relevant?
paragraphs.zh: 我在想，随着 CPU 生态系统的变化，这会不会成为我们开始更在意的一个领域，因为服务器上的能效正越来越成为一个问题，而 ARM 似乎正是能效最出众的地方。你觉得这会变得更相关吗？

paragraphs.en: I think the comments about register allocator also apply to the AMD64 backend where we know that the register allocator has some issues, and there is a lot of potential improvements here. And there's lots of interesting research on register allocation and register allocators, some of which is quite old but still applies.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得关于寄存器分配器的那些说法也适用于 AMD64 后端，我们知道寄存器分配器有一些问题，这里有很大的改进潜力。关于寄存器分配和寄存器分配器有很多有意思的研究，其中有些相当老了但仍然适用。

paragraphs.en: I'm glad that came across as a positive experience because it's something that we try and keep baked into the process here, where the idea is that we want to be improving OCaml and working with the upstream community. And that's something we want all of our compiler engineers here to engage with. So, how do you find the experience of working with the broader OCaml community?
paragraphs.zh: 我很高兴这给人留下了正面的体验，因为这是我们这里努力一直贯穿在流程中的东西，我们的想法是想要改进 OCaml 并与上游社区合作。这也是我们希望这里所有编译器工程师都去参与的事情。那么，你觉得和更广泛的 OCaml 社区合作的体验怎么样？

paragraphs.en: We see many examples internally where we see a way to improve them. We see that, for example, like when we were working on FDO, that's an example where we needed to add functionality to the compiler. And also the experience of working with the community is mostly positive. The community is welcoming, and the maintainers that I interacted with were very responsive.
paragraphs.zh: 我们在内部看到很多例子，看到一种改进它们的方法。比如我们在做 FDO 的时候，那就是一个我们需要给编译器添加功能的例子。而且和社区合作的体验大体上也是正面的。社区很欢迎人，我打过交道的维护者都非常积极回应。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, I suspect this is a place where you probably just need to build some better tooling, among other things, to make it easier to demonstrate the effect of changes in the compiler, to make it easier to share reproducible benchmarks. Do you have thoughts about how to make that better?
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，我猜这是一个你大概只需要构建一些更好的工具的地方，除了其他事情之外，让展示编译器中改动的效果更容易，让分享可复现的基准测试更容易。关于怎么把这个做得更好，你有什么想法吗？

paragraphs.en: I think the core for this objection is in the approach to code generation for OCaml. The backend is not very modular. As I said, this is a known issue in the OCaml community. And this means that implementing something like a new optimization or a new analysis is difficult, because you need to go and add a pass and maintain it in all backends.
paragraphs.zh: 我觉得这个异议的核心在于 OCaml 代码生成的方式上。后端不是很模块化。正如我说的，这在 OCaml 社区是个已知的问题。这就意味着，实现像一个新优化或一个新分析这样的东西很困难，因为你得去添加一个 pass，并在所有后端里维护它。

paragraphs.en: Yeah, this is maybe a case where modularity is just a thing that can help. Like, I think one of the things that modularity does is it makes it easier to experiment. And if you can experiment cheaply, then you can more easily demonstrate that something is an improvement. And that demonstration is exactly the thing you need to get community buy-in.
paragraphs.zh: 嗯，这也许是一个模块化恰恰能帮上忙的情况。比如，我觉得模块化所做的一件事是让实验变得更容易。而如果你能廉价地做实验，那你就能更容易地证明某个东西是一种改进。而那种证明恰恰是你赢得社区认同所需要的东西。

paragraphs.en: Indeed, and also getting feedback. So, thumbs up from other users of OCaml would be easier if it didn't require maintaining the code in all backends. And that's kind of the point of having an intermediate representation.
paragraphs.zh: 确实，而且还有获得反馈这件事。所以，如果不需要在所有后端里维护代码，要得到 OCaml 其他用户点赞认可就会更容易。而这某种意义上正是拥有一个中间表示的意义所在。

paragraphs.en: Here we're talking about feedback-directed compiler construction.
paragraphs.zh: 我们这里说的是反馈导向的编译器构建。

paragraphs.en: There we go.
paragraphs.zh: 就是这么回事。

paragraphs.en:
Different kinds of feedback.
 Okay, well, I think we are, at this point, out of time, but thank you so much for coming. This was super interesting.
paragraphs.zh: 各种不同的反馈。好，我想我们到这儿时间差不多了，但非常感谢你过来。这真是太有意思了。

paragraphs.en: Thank you.
paragraphs.zh: 谢谢你。

paragraphs.en: You can find links to some of the things that we talked about, including Greta's work on feedback-directed optimization, on the Signals & Threads website. We'll also have a transcript up there for this episode, as we do for all of our episodes, as well as links to other related episodes.
paragraphs.zh: 你可以在 Signals & Threads 网站上找到我们聊到的一些内容的链接，包括 Greta 在反馈导向优化方面的工作。我们也会在那里放上本期节目的文字记录，就像我们对所有节目所做的那样，还有其他相关节目的链接。
